author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
What It is to Be Delivered from the Body of This Death.
title: What It is to Be Delivered from the Body of This Death.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Chapter 23 [XI.]—What It is to Be Delivered from the Body of This Death.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For when he says also, “Who shall deliver me from the body of this death?” who can deny that when the apostle said this he was still in the body of this death?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And certainly the wicked are not delivered from this, to whom the same bodies are returned for eternal torment.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore, to be delivered from the body of this death is to be healed of all the weakness of fleshly lust, and to receive the body, not for penalty, but for glory.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: With this passage also those words are sufficiently in harmony: “Ourselves also, which have the first-fruits of the Spirit, even we ourselves groan within ourselves, waiting for the adoption, the redemption, of our body.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For surely we groan with that groaning wherein we say, “O wretched man that I am! who shall deliver me from the body of this death?”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: That also where he says, “For what I do, I know not;” what else is it than: “I will not, I do not approve, I do not consent, I do not do”?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Otherwise it is contrary to what he said above, “By the law is the knowledge of sin,” and, “I had not known sin but by the law,” and, “Sin, that it might appear sin, worked death in me by that which is good.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For how did he know sin, of which he was ignorant, by the law?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: How does sin which is not known appear?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore it is said, “I know not,” for “I do not,” because I myself commit it with no consent of mine; in the same way in which the Lord will say to the wicked, “I know you not,” although, beyond a doubt, nothing can be hid from Him; and as it is said, “Him who had not known sin,” which means who had not done sin, for He had not known what He condemned.
